title: Habakkuk
author: Habakkuk the prophet (nothing more is known of him)
date: ~609–598 BC (after Josiah's death, before or during Babylon's rise)
setting: Judah under Jehoiakim, as Babylon is rising as the new world power. Habakkuk dares to complain directly to God about injustice and receives an unsettling answer.
purpose: To wrestle with the problem of God's justice — if God is sovereign, why does he tolerate evil in his own people, and why does he use an even more wicked nation to judge them? The book arrives at sovereign trust: 'the righteous shall live by his faith' (2:4).
themes: Honest prayer: bringing complaints to God
The righteous shall live by faith (2:4 — quoted 3x in NT)
God's sovereignty over history, including wicked instruments
Trust and worship despite circumstances (ch. 3 psalm)

key_people.name: Habakkuk
key_people.role: Prophet of uncertain date, likely late 7th century BC; his book is structured as a dialogue with God — one of only two prophetic books (with Jonah) where the prophet's interior life is the primary subject matter

context: Habakkuk prophesied around 605 BC, after the Battle of Carchemish when Babylon was clearly emerging as the dominant world power. The book addresses a theological crisis: God tells Habakkuk he is raising up the Babylonians to judge Judah, but Habakkuk protests that using a more wicked nation to judge a less wicked nation seems unjust. The book is a profound engagement with the problem of theodicy — why does God allow (or use) the triumph of the wicked over the righteous?
christ_connection: Habakkuk 2:4 ('the righteous shall live by his faith') is the text on which three NT letters turn: Romans 1:17, Galatians 3:11, and Hebrews 10:38. Paul quotes it to establish the principle of justification by faith that was true even in the OT — righteousness before God has always been through faith, not works-righteousness. The use of this verse in three different NT contexts shows its theological centrality: it is the hinge on which the entire gospel turns. The 'vision' that Habakkuk waits for is ultimately fulfilled in Christ's death and resurrection.

key_verses.note: 'The righteous shall live by his faith' — quoted in Romans 1:17, Galatians 3:11, and Hebrews 10:38 as the OT foundation for the doctrine of justification by faith
'For the earth will be filled with the knowledge of the glory of the LORD as the waters cover the sea' — the ultimate horizon of history, even when the Babylonians appear to be winning

'Though the fig tree should not blossom... yet I will rejoice in the LORD' — the theological climax: faith not as feeling but as a deliberate act of trust when circumstances provide no visible reason for hope

themes_detail.title: The Prophet's Complaint and God's Surprising Answer
themes_detail.text: Habakkuk's first complaint (1:2–4) is that God ignores the injustice and violence in Judah. God's answer (1:5–11) is astonishing: he is raising up the Babylonians to deal with it. Habakkuk's second complaint (1:12–2:1) is that this answer creates a worse problem: how can a holy God use a nation more wicked than Judah as his instrument of judgment? This structure — complaint, surprising answer, deeper complaint — reflects a theology of prayer that takes God's apparent moral inconsistency seriously rather than suppressing the question.

themes_detail.title: Living by Faith When Circumstances Contradict
themes_detail.text: Habakkuk 2:4 — 'the righteous shall live by his faith' — emerges from a context where the righteous have no visible reason for confidence. Babylon is winning; the proud are swallowing those more righteous than themselves; the appointed time seems distant. 'Faith' here is not intellectual assent but practical trust: the orientation of one's entire life around what God has said rather than what one's circumstances suggest. This is the faith that Paul develops in Romans as the only basis for righteousness before God — a faith that was always the right response to God's word, from Abraham onward.

themes_detail.title: The Woes Against Babylon and God's Moral Order
themes_detail.text: God's answer to Habakkuk's second complaint (2:2–20) is a series of five woe-oracles against Babylon covering greed, exploitation, violence, shame, and idolatry. These woes establish that God has not suspended his moral order to use Babylon as his instrument — Babylon will itself be judged for its own sins. The principle 'the earth will be filled with the knowledge of the glory of the LORD as the waters cover the sea' (2:14) is the eschatological answer to Habakkuk's theodicy: the present apparent triumph of the wicked is penultimate, not ultimate.

themes_detail.title: The Psalm of Trusting Faith
themes_detail.text: Habakkuk 3 is a formal psalm, complete with musical directions, that is also a theological resolution to the book's tensions. The vision of God coming in theophanic power (3:3–15) grounds Habakkuk's trust: the God who intervened in the Exodus can intervene again. The climactic verses (3:17–19) are among the most remarkable in Scripture: even if every material blessing is stripped away — no crop, no flock, no food — 'yet I will rejoice in the LORD.' This is not stoicism but eschatological faith: God is a source of joy that external circumstances cannot negate.
